student_message: Really? I was so scared. Are we going to do another shape now? Sorry, did I mess up?
final_message:
Math can be hard sometimes, so it is perfectly normal to feel a little scared, Kabir! You didn't mess up at all. In fact, you did a wonderful job noticing that the two sides of the letter A match. Making mistakes is just a normal part of learning, and you are doing great! 💛 

Let's gently look at a new shape together. Can you picture the letter H? The letter A has a line of symmetry that goes straight up and down to make a perfect left-right mirror. The letter H is extra special because it has that same up-and-down line, plus another line going straight across the middle! That means the top and bottom halves are perfect mirror images, too. Can you see how folding the letter H across its middle makes the top and bottom match perfectly? 🌟

orchestrator_intent.goal: Reassure Kabir he didn't mess up and gently introduce a new shape.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Kabir is expressing fear and anxiety about making mistakes. Encouragement is needed to build his confidence before continuing the lesson.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: The session covered lines of symmetry in shapes. The student successfully understood that a line of symmetry goes straight down the middle, making both sides match. However, the student struggled significantly with math anxiety, frequent self-doubt, and a fear of making mistakes. Currently, the student accurately grasps the foundational concept of symmetry but lacks confidence, requiring continuous emotional support and reassurance to progress.
